Anatomy · Objective 38A

Head & Neck Organization

Skull plan · Cranial & facial bones · Cervical vertebrae · Neck muscle groups · Cervical triangles

Overview Skull Cervical Spine Muscles Triangles Rapid Review
1

At-a-Glance Organization

Total skull bones
22
Neurocranium + Viscerocranium
Cranial vault
8
Neurocranium — encloses the brain
Facial bones
14
Viscerocranium — builds the face
Cervical vertebrae
C1–C7
Support & move the head
Big-picture frame: Brain vault (neurocranium) above → facial skeleton (viscerocranium) in front → cervical spine below → triangles as the surface map of the neck.
2

Head & Skull Organization

Split the skull into its two major components first — the bone list follows naturally from there.

Neurocranium — the brain case (8 bones)

The cranial vault that contains and protects the brain. The major visible bones on a lateral view are the frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital, and sphenoid regions.

BoneCountRegion / Role
Frontal1Forehead and anterior cranial vault
Parietal2Roof and sides of the skull
Temporal2Lateral skull and ear region
Occipital1Posterior skull and cranial base
Sphenoid1"Keystone" of the cranial base
Ethmoid1Anterior cranial base and nasal cavity
Study tip: The lateral view mainly shows outer bones — remember to include the sphenoid and ethmoid to reach the full count of 8.

Viscerocranium — the facial skeleton (14 bones)

The series of bony arches surrounding the oral and nasal passageways from the face to the pharynx.

BoneCountRegion
Nasal2Bridge of nose
Lacrimal2Medial orbit
Inferior nasal concha2Lateral nasal wall
Maxilla2Upper jaw and midface
Palatine2Posterior hard palate
Zygomatic2Cheek and lateral orbit
Vomer1Nasal septum
Mandible1Lower jaw
Clean split: 8 cranial bones protect the brain · 14 facial bones build the face = 22 total.
3

Cervical Spine — Bony Support of the Neck

The 7 cervical vertebrae (C1–C7) support the skull and allow head movement. Three of them are distinctively shaped and high-yield for exams.

C1

Atlas

Lacks a body and spinous process. Forms a bony ring that articulates with the skull's occipital condyles.

"Atlas holds the world up"
C2

Axis

Has the dens (odontoid process) — a bony peg projecting upward into the C1 ring, enabling head rotation.

"Axis turns it"
C7

Vertebra Prominens

Long, prominent spinous process easily palpated at the base of the neck — the key surface landmark.

"C7 sticks out"
4

Neck Organization by Muscle Groups

The neck is divided into major muscle families. Knowing these groups builds the spatial map needed before tackling vessels and nerves.

GroupKey MusclesPrimary Function
SuperficialPlatysma, SCMFacial/neck tension; rotation & flexion of the head
SuprahyoidMylohyoid, digastric, geniohyoid, stylohyoidElevate the hyoid and floor of the mouth
Infrahyoid (strap)Sternohyoid, sternothyroid, thyrohyoid, omohyoidDepress the hyoid and laryngeal structures
ScalenesAnterior, middle, posteriorLateral neck flexion; elevate ribs 1–2
PrevertebralLongus capitis, longus colliDeep anterior flexors of head and neck
SuboccipitalRectus capitis, obliquus capitis groupFine extension & rotation at C1–C2
5

Cervical Triangles — Surface Map of the Neck

The neck divides into an anterior triangle and a posterior triangle, each subdivided into smaller named spaces used to locate vessels, nerves, glands, and muscles.

Why it matters: Triangles turn a complex 3D region into a navigable map for anatomy lab, physical exam, and imaging.

Anterior Cervical Triangle

Between the midline, the hyoid/mandible superiorly, and the sternocleidomastoid laterally. Subdivided into four named triangles:

Anterior triangle

Submental

  • Anterior bellies of both digastrics + hyoid body
  • Floor: mylohyoid
  • Submental lymph nodes, anterior jugular vein tributaries
Anterior triangle

Submandibular

  • Mandible + anterior & posterior bellies of digastric
  • Submandibular gland, facial artery & vein
  • CN XII (hypoglossal), nerve to mylohyoid
Anterior triangle

Carotid ⭐

  • SCM + posterior belly of digastric + omohyoid
  • Carotid sheath (IJV, CN X, common carotid)
  • Carotid bifurcation, ansa cervicalis, CN XII
Anterior triangle

Muscular

  • SCM + omohyoid + midline
  • Infrahyoid (strap) muscles
  • Thyroid, parathyroids, larynx, trachea, esophagus

Posterior Cervical Triangle

Between the posterior SCM, the anterior trapezius, and the clavicle. The omohyoid divides it into two parts:

Posterior triangle

Occipital

  • Posterior SCM + anterior trapezius + omohyoid
  • CN XI (accessory nerve)
  • Cervical plexus branches, upper brachial plexus
Posterior triangle

Supraclavicular

  • Posterior SCM + omohyoid + clavicle
  • Subclavian artery, brachial plexus trunks
  • Nerve to subclavius, lymph nodes
6

Rapid Review — Answer Without Looking

Click each question to reveal the answer.

1
How is the skull divided into two parts?▾
Neurocranium (8 bones) protects the brain; viscerocranium (14 bones) forms the face. 8 + 14 = 22 total.
2
Name the 8 neurocranial bones.▾
Frontal (1), Parietal (2), Temporal (2), Occipital (1), Sphenoid (1), Ethmoid (1).
3
Name the 14 facial bones.▾
Nasal (2), Lacrimal (2), Inferior nasal concha (2), Maxilla (2), Palatine (2), Zygomatic (2), Vomer (1), Mandible (1).
4
What is unique about C1 (atlas)?▾
Lacks a body and spinous process. Forms a ring that articulates with the skull's occipital condyles.
5
What is unique about C2 (axis)?▾
Has the dens (odontoid process) — a peg projecting into the C1 ring — enabling rotational head movement.
6
What is unique about C7?▾
Long, palpable spinous process at the base of the neck — the key surface landmark for counting vertebral levels.
7
What are the two major neck triangles?▾
Anterior cervical triangle and posterior cervical triangle.
8
What are the 4 subdivisions of the anterior triangle?▾
Submental · Submandibular · Carotid · Muscular.
9
What divides the posterior triangle?▾
The omohyoid muscle — into the occipital triangle (upper) and supraclavicular triangle (lower).
10
Which triangle has the carotid bifurcation?▾
Carotid triangle — also contains the carotid sheath (IJV, CN X, common carotid), ansa cervicalis, and CN XII.
7

Exam-Style MCQs

Use these to check the skull-cervical-triangle landmarks the way an anatomy stem usually tests them.

1
A fracture through the odontoid process severely limits rotation of the head relative to the neck. Which vertebra contains the odontoid process?
A. Atlas (C1)
B. Axis (C2)
C. C6
D. C7
▾
Answer: B. Axis (C2). The dens, or odontoid process, projects from the axis and serves as the pivot for atlantoaxial rotation. The atlas lacks a vertebral body and spinous process, while C6 and C7 are lower cervical vertebrae without a dens.
2
During a neck dissection, the surgeon exposes the carotid bifurcation along with CN XII and the ansa cervicalis. Which triangle has been opened?
A. Submental triangle
B. Submandibular triangle
C. Carotid triangle
D. Muscular triangle
▾
Answer: C. Carotid triangle. The carotid triangle is the high-yield anterior-triangle subdivision containing the carotid sheath, carotid bifurcation, CN XII, and the ansa cervicalis. The other anterior-triangle subdivisions do not center on the carotid vessels.
✕
